title: LLM Fundamentals
description: What are LLMs, how do they work, and what does it take to run one locally?

stops.title: What Is an LLM?
stops.narrative:
A [Large Language Model](node:llm) is a neural network trained on massive text corpora to predict the next [token](node:tokens) in a sequence. That simple objective — next-token prediction — produces surprisingly capable systems that can write code, reason about problems, and follow complex instructions.

LLMs are built on the [Transformer](node:transformer) architecture and their capability roughly scales with [parameter count](node:parameters). Running them locally means all computation stays on your machine — no data leaves, no API costs, no rate limits.

stops.title: Parameters and Memory
stops.narrative:
[Parameters](node:parameters) are the learnable weights in the neural network — the 'knowledge' the model acquired during training. A 70B model has 70 billion of them.

Each parameter is stored as a number. At full precision (FP16), each takes 2 bytes. So a 70B model needs ~140GB just for weights. This is why [quantization](node:quantization) is essential for local inference — it compresses parameters to 4-8 bits each, cutting memory by 2-4x.

[VRAM](node:vram) (or unified memory) is the critical constraint: it determines the largest model you can fit.

stops.title: Quantization: Making Models Fit
stops.narrative:
[Quantization](node:quantization) reduces the precision of each [parameter](node:parameters). Instead of 16 bits per weight, you use 4-8 bits. A 70B model goes from ~140GB to ~42GB at 4-bit.

The key formats:
- [GGUF](node:gguf) — the standard for desktop inference ([llama.cpp](node:llama-cpp), [Ollama](node:ollama)). K-quants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, etc.) offer the best quality-per-bit.
- [GPTQ](node:gptq) / [AWQ](node:awq) — GPU-optimized formats used by [vLLM](node:vllm) and production servers.
- [EXL2](node:exl2) / [EXL3](node:exl3) — per-layer mixed precision, highest quality at extreme compression.

Modern 4-bit models retain 90-95% of full-precision quality.

stops.title: How Inference Works
stops.narrative:
[Inference](node:inference) is running the model to generate output. It has two phases:

**Prefill**: Processing your input prompt. This is compute-bound — the [GPU](node:gpu) crunches through all input tokens in parallel. Fast on GPUs with high TFLOPS.

**Decode**: Generating tokens one by one. This is [memory-bandwidth](node:memory-bandwidth) bound — each new token requires reading ALL model weights from memory. On [Strix Halo](node:strix-halo) (~215 GB/s), a 42GB model generates at ~5 tok/s.

The [KV cache](node:kv-cache) stores attention state and grows with [context length](node:context-window), adding to memory pressure.

stops.title: GPU, VRAM, and Bandwidth
stops.narrative:
[GPUs](node:gpu) are essential for LLM inference because they excel at matrix multiplication — the core operation in [transformers](node:transformer).

Three specs matter:
- **[VRAM](node:vram)**: How much model fits. Discrete GPUs: 8-48GB. Unified memory ([Strix Halo](node:strix-halo)): 128GB.
- **[Memory bandwidth](node:memory-bandwidth)**: Determines tok/s during decode. Discrete: 500-1000+ GB/s. Unified: ~215 GB/s.
- **Compute (TFLOPS)**: Determines prefill speed.

NVIDIA dominates with [CUDA](node:cuda). AMD users rely on [Vulkan](node:vulkan) or [ROCm](node:rocm). [NPUs](node:npu) are a new option for efficient smaller-model inference.

stops.title: Mixture of Experts: More Brain, Less Compute
stops.narrative:
[Mixture of Experts](node:moe) is an architecture trick: the model has many [parameters](node:parameters) but only activates a subset for each [token](node:tokens). [Qwen3-Coder-Next](node:qwen3-coder-next) has 80B total but only 3B active — it reasons like a much larger model while computing like a small one.

The catch: ALL parameters must fit in [VRAM](node:vram). This is where unified memory shines — on [Strix Halo](node:strix-halo), the dormant experts sit in the same memory pool with zero transfer overhead.

stops.title: Tool Calling: From Text to Action
stops.narrative:
[Tool calling](node:tool-calling) transforms an [LLM](node:llm) from a text generator into an agent. The model outputs structured JSON describing which function to call — read a file, run a test, edit code. [Claude Code](node:claude-code) relies heavily on this.

Not all local models support tool calling reliably. It requires strong instruction following and consistent JSON generation. [Qwen3-Coder-Next](node:qwen3-coder-next) and [Devstral 2](node:devstral-2) have the best support among open models. This is the main gap between local and cloud models today.
